turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to destructure a Vec into a first element and the rest, but my pattern keeps failing in production. It feels like Rust is being weird about how it handles indices.

It's not really weird; that's just how Rust's slice patterns operate under the hood when matching on Vecs or slices. When you use [first, rest @ ..], the compiler treats first as a single element and rest as a sub-slice starting after that index. The pattern matcher validates that the input length is at least one more than what your pattern requires before attempting binding.
Okay, so I get it works if the vector has enough items, but what exactly happens to rest when I bind it? Does Rust actually create a new slice type or does it just reference existing memory?

Rust creates a fresh sub-slice that is owned by the matched value. The @ binding operator ensures rest captures exactly the remaining elements after first, and this resulting slice has its own internal length and capacity constraints derived from the original Vec's backing data.

Wait, I pushed back on that earlier explanation because I thought it was just a view. But if rest is actually a real slice with its own bounds, then what happens when I try to use rest without cloning? Does the runtime panic or does Rust's ownership model prevent misuse?

The compiler enforces strict safety rules here. If you attempt to access rest beyond its valid bounds, you'll get a compile-time error because the slice pattern guarantees that rest only contains elements after first's index. There is no runtime panic for this specific issue since Rust catches boundary violations at compile time through the pattern matching logic.
That makes sense, but what if I want to use a fixed-length slice pattern instead? Like extracting exactly three elements and discarding the rest without using the @ operator?

You can use [a, b, c] for fixed-length extraction when matching on Vec<i32> or similar types. This matches exactly three consecutive items and leaves the remaining elements untouched in the original collection. The pattern enforces that there are at least three elements available before binding succeeds.

One more thing I need to clarify: when I match on a slice reference &mut [i32] instead of Vec<i32>, do the same rules apply? Does the pattern matching behave differently with borrowed data versus owned data?

The behavior is identical for both owned and borrowed slices. The difference lies in mutability requirements—if you match on &mut [i32], your code must declare the variable as mutable, but the pattern matching logic regarding index validation and element binding remains consistent regardless of ownership.
